evidence: The paper uses random forest (ranger package) to predict BaP exposure and larval behavior from microbiome taxa, employing a two-stage approach: "To determine which taxa putatively underlie the variation...we again used random forest to prioritize candidate taxa that were subsequently statistically evaluated using multivariate regression." Critically, they validate with held-out test sets (30% test, reporting 16-20% error rates), regression p-values, and germ-free experiments. Importantly, they avoid magnitude/ranking claims, instead stating that selected taxa "could be investigated further in the future" and acknowledging results "may primarily be driven by a few outliers." Random forest importance serves only for variable filtering, not substantive conclusions; final findings rest on PERMANOVA diversity tests, linear regression p-values, and experimental microbiome state comparisons.
